items.title: Track matrix shapes explicitly
items.avoid: Relying on visual intuition or trial-and-error when combining matrices, hoping the operations will just work.
items.prefer: Write down or assert the shape of each matrix (m x n) and reason about whether an operation is defined before coding it.
items.why: Shape reasoning mirrors the algebraic rules and prevents subtle bugs in both math derivations and ML code.
items.example: Before computing y = X @ w, note that X is (batch_size x num_features) and w is (num_features x 1), so the product makes sense and yields a (batch_size x 1) vector.

items.title: Separate data matrices from parameter matrices
items.avoid: Reusing the same variable names for both datasets and weight matrices or mixing their roles conceptually.
items.prefer: Use clear names and mental models: X for data, W for weights, b for bias, and keep track of their shapes and roles.
items.why: Clarity about what each matrix represents makes it easier to debug mismatched operations and reason about transformations.
items.example: Instead of writing `A = A @ A` in a neural net, write `H = X @ W1` and `Y = H @ W2` so you can see that data flows from X through weight matrices W1 and W2.

items.title: Use transpose deliberately, not reflexively
items.avoid: Adding transpose operations randomly until code runs without errors.
items.prefer: Add a transpose only when it has a clear mathematical meaning, such as switching between row-wise and column-wise conventions or forming inner products.
items.why: Random transposes can hide deeper shape mismatches and make your derivations harder to follow.
items.example: If your convention is that each example is a row, keep that convention throughout instead of transposing the data matrix back and forth.
